summary: DHH argues that the primary benefit of having senior employees is the managerial bandwidth they free up by operating independently. Juniors require frequent feedback and direction, which consumes scarce managerial attention. Senior employees should be 'managers of one' who take rough direction and deliver results without hand-holding. The ideal organization mixes just enough junior talent to prepare for the future with enough senior talent to handle the present, while keeping managerial bandwidth deliberately scarce to prevent process churn.
key_points: Juniors are judged on effort and require frequent feedback; seniors are judged on outcomes and should operate independently
Excess managerial bandwidth is dangerous — it leads to constant churning of plans and processes
Senior employees who consume more bandwidth than they contribute back are failing at their role
Organizations should use 'moonlighting managers' who'd rather be building to naturally constrain management overhead
The right mix of junior and senior talent should yield just enough productive bandwidth — not too much, not too little
money_quotes: Juniors are judged on effort, seniors are judged on outcomes.
There's nothing more dangerous than excess managerial bandwidth.
If you want just enough management, but absolutely no more, hire managers who'd rather be building.
The best of them all encompass the ideal of being managers of one.
Capable to finding the right path independently to a destination worth heading. Interested in taking fuzzy problems and turning them into crisp solutions.
You should be wary of adding more lest you end up with too much.
The goal of the organization is to mix just enough junior talent that it's preparing for the future, with enough of the seniority needed to tend to the present.
key_insight: Senior employees should be net producers of organizational bandwidth rather than consumers of it, and excess managerial capacity is more dangerous than too little.
